verbatim_text: So we know that autoregressive decoding, there's a randomization process. So you want to maybe make parallel attempts, and that will give you some diverse responses. But turns out that that kind of diversity is pretty limited in scope. It's mostly where the problem is trying to, it's more of a brute force approach, where sometimes one of the rollouts end up getting lucky. And that's the one that you end up learning. But this kind of exploration we're looking at is something that goes beyond just like parallel sampling. So in general, you can think about, OK, one way of exploring is sequentially, like get them all to generate longer. The other way of exploration is parallel, you get parallel sequences. This sort of cuts across in some sense, because here what you're trying to do is looking at parallel rollouts of like past attempts, summarizing them in many cases, and trying to use that as a preconditioner for your sequential sampling. So you can look at like multiple past GRPO rollouts and summarize them into a sufficient state or a sufficient statistic, that now when you condition on it and you generate responses sequentially, you can improve exploration. So it sort of cuts across. So is that context given to each, like the same context? Yes, it's the same context given to each. So the difference is still in sampling?
